text: Vanity of vanities, all is vanity, except to love God and serve Him alone.
What doth it profit thee to enter into deep discussion concerning the Holy Trinity, if thou lack humility, and be thus displeasing to the Trinity?
Better of a surety is a lowly peasant who serveth God, than a proud philosopher who watcheth the stars and neglecteth the knowledge of himself.
He who knoweth himself well is vile in his own sight, and delighteth not in the praises of men.
If I knew all things that are in the world, and were not in charity, what would it profit me before God, who is to judge me according to my deeds?
What doth it profit thee to discourse profoundly concerning the Holy Trinity, if thou be void of humility, and be thus displeasing to the Trinity?
Truly great learning will not profit thee if thou live wickedly and displease God.
I had rather feel contrition than be skilful in the definition thereof.
Blessed is the man who is instructed by Truth itself, not by signs and passing words, but as It is in Itself.
He to whom the Eternal Word speaketh is set free from many opinions.
Many words satisfy not the soul, but a good life refresheth the mind, and a pure conscience giveth great confidence towards God.
We must not trust every word of others or every feeling within ourselves, but cautiously and patiently try the matter, whether it be of God.
Alas! how quickly can the glory of the world pass away!
Great peace have they who love God, and no occasion of stumbling can come to them.
Vain is the man who putteth his trust in men or in any created thing.
It is vanity to desire a long life, and to have little care for a good life.
It is vanity to mind only the present life, and not to look forward to those things which are to come.
It is vanity to love that which quickly passeth away, and not to hasten where everlasting joy abideth.
Who is so wise as to have full knowledge of all things? Therefore trust not too much to thine own opinion, but be ready also to hear the opinions of others.
He who hath peace of conscience and careth for no man, is of good hope.
What doth it profit us to have a thorough knowledge of the whole Bible, if we be void of the grace of God, and without His love?
If thou wilt withdraw thyself from speaking unnecessarily and from idle visiting, as also from listening to news and gossip, thou shalt find thy time sufficient for good meditation.
Temptation is troublesome and grievous to all men; but it is very profitable to a man in the way of humility, since by it a man is humbled, purified, and instructed.
In the beginning of temptation it is easier to overcome, because the enemy, being refused entrance, straightway departeth.
Resist thy passions and thou shalt conquer all bad habits. Vain security bringeth much peril, and overconfidence in one's own safety is the cause of much ruin.
There be many who seek knowledge, but there are but few who care to live humbly and obediently.
Bear cheerfully the wrongs that are done thee, and frame not thyself to excel in human wisdom.
We naturally all desire knowledge, but what profiteth knowledge without the fear of God?
A meek spirit, yea even a peasant who serveth God, is better than a proud philosopher who studieth the course of the stars.
Judge not that ye be not judged. In judging others a man toils in vain, often errs, and easily sins; but in judging and examining himself he always labours with profit.
The wise lover of truth lifteth his eyes, not to the giver, but to the Lord.
Blessed is the man whose heart is set free from outward things, and who cheerfully beareth the burden of his infirmities.
Sorrow for sin is not unprofitable when it giveth birth to compunction.
It is vanity to follow the lust of the flesh, and to desire things for which thou must afterwards suffer grievous punishment.
How quickly doth the glory of the world pass away! Would that the life of these men had been as holy as it was reputed learned!
Great is the difference between a wise man illuminated from above, and a learned man who studieth the sciences for vainglory.
Rest from inordinate desire of knowledge, for therein is found much distraction and deceit.
It is vanity to desire riches, and to place thy hope therein.
Better is it certainly that thou shouldest be an unknown poor man, than that thou shouldest be a philosopher who watcheth the stars and neglecteth the knowledge of himself.

There is naturally in every man a desire to know, but what profiteth knowledge without the fear of God? Humble is that rustic who serveth God, rather than the proud philosopher who watcheth the stars.
The kingdom of God is within you, saith the Lord. Turn thee with thy whole heart unto the Lord, and forsake this miserable world, and thy soul shall find rest.
If thy heart were right, then every created thing would be to thee a mirror of life and a book of holy doctrine.
Blessed are the pure in heart, for they shall see God.
He is truly great who hath great charity. He is truly great who is little in his own sight, and who counteth the height of honour as nothing.
He is truly wise who counteth all earthly things as dung that he may win Christ.
He is truly learned who doeth the will of God and forsaketh his own will.
Peace in this world doth not consist in being without trouble, but in having the heart fully set upon God, and in resting quietly in His will.
The man of good will turneth all things to his advantage.
A pure heart penetrateth heaven and hell. Such as each one is inwardly, so he judgeth outwardly.
If there be joy in the world, surely the man of pure heart possesseth it.
Trust not overmuch in thine own opinion, but be ready to hear the opinion of others.
When a man is troubled and tempted and afflicted with evil thoughts, he then understandeth better the great need he hath of God, without Whom he can do nothing that is good.
Love feeleth no burden, thinketh nothing of labours, attempteth what is above its strength, pleadeth no excuse of impossibility; for it thinketh all things possible for itself, and all things lawful.

Love is swift, sincere, pious, pleasant, gentle, strong, patient, faithful, prudent, long-suffering, manly, and never seeking her own; for wheresoever a man seeketh his own, there he falleth from love.
The more humble a man is in himself, and the more obedient toward God, the wiser will he be in all things, and the more shall his soul be at peace.
God walketh with the humble; He revealeth Himself to the lowly; He giveth understanding to little ones; He discloseth His meaning to pure minds, and hideth His grace from the curious and the proud.
He who followeth Me shall not walk in darkness, saith the Lord. These are the words of Christ, and they teach us how far we must imitate His life and character.
Rest from inordinate desire of knowledge, for therein is found much distraction and deceit.
He who knoweth himself well is vile in his own sight, and delighteth not in the praises of men.
Blessed is the soul which heareth the Lord speaking within her, and receiveth from His mouth the word of consolation.
Blessed are the eyes which are closed to outward things, and are opened to inward things.
I am the Way, the Truth, and the Life. Without the Way there is no going; without the Truth, there is no knowing; without the Life, there is no living.
I am the inward Teacher, the Instructor of truth, the Searcher of the heart, the Understander of thoughts, the Promoter of virtues, the Rewarder of the deserving.
Let this be thy chief endeavour, this thy prayer, this thy desire, that thou mayest be stripped of all selfishness, and with entire simplicity follow Jesus only.
We judge many things amiss because we seek our own convenience, and not what is pleasing to God.
Truth speaketh within us, but it doth not always resound outwardly.
When a man desireth a thing too eagerly, he becometh ill at ease. The proud and the avaricious man are never at rest, whilst the poor and humble of heart dwell in a multitude of peace.
Vain is the man who putteth his trust in men or in any created thing.
The humble knowledge of thyself is a surer way to God than the deepest search after learning.
All is vanity but to love God and serve Him alone.
Many words satisfy not the soul; but a good life refresheth the mind, and a pure conscience giveth great confidence before God.
He who loveth God from the heart feareth neither death, nor punishment, nor judgment, nor hell, because perfect love giveth sure access to God.
Nothing so thoroughly purgeth the soul and enlighteneth the mind as the sincere practice of the love of God and entire humility towards Him.
Learn to break thine own will in many things; then thou shalt be able to keep peace with others.
He who hath peace with himself careth for no man, and is troubled by nothing.
If thou wouldst know what helpeth thee, and what hurteth thee, consider what maketh thee more humble in spirit, and more subject to God.
The knowledge of thyself is more profitable than the knowledge of the stars.
When thou art troubled, it is then that thou art nearest to blessing; for through the tribulation cometh the visitation of God.
Thy peace shall be in much patience. The patient man doth enjoy much peace.
Blessed are those who have peace in themselves, and who are not troubled by the varying events of the world.
Be of good courage, therefore, and cast not away thy confidence; but pray earnestly, lest thou grow weary and faint.
My son, thou canst not possess perfect charity, unless first there be thoroughly subdued in thee the love of thyself.
He whom I love, him I reprove and chasten; therefore receive tribulations with cheerfulness.
One thing is needful, and that is, to forsake thyself and all things, and to think only of eternal things and of pleasing God.
My son, I am the Lord, who giveth strength in the day of tribulation. Come unto Me, when it is not well with thee.
When a man hath arrived at this, that he seeketh comfort from no created thing, then doth God begin perfectly to comfort him; then also doth he find that without God all things displease him.
I am He who on a sudden giveth more to the humble mind than the teacher who hath learned much can obtain by long study.
Rest from inordinate longing after knowledge, because many distractions are therein and much deceit.
He who is well instructed knoweth well that all things are vanity except to love and serve God.
All is vanity but to love God and to serve Him only, and to despise the world.
I will speak to my Lord, though I am but dust and ashes. I am not able to bear it unless Thou strengthen me, unless Thou comfort me with Thy heavenly consolation.
My son, thou canst not always continue in the very fervent desire of virtue, nor remain on the highest level of contemplation, but thou must needs sometimes descend to lower things.
Nature seeketh to know secrets and to hear news; it desireth to appear and to experience many things through the senses; it wisheth to be acknowledged and to do those things which procure praise.
In truth all is vanity, except to love God and to serve Him alone. He is truly great who counteth himself small, and is not troubled by the highest honour.
What is it to thee whether such an one be this or that? Thou shalt give an account of thyself, not of others. Why therefore dost thou entangle thyself?
Commit thyself wholly to God, and think not of what will occur, but trust fully in God and in His mercy.
Come unto Me all ye that labour and are heavy laden, and I will refresh you. The bread which I give is My flesh, which I give for the life of the world.
Receive ye all of this, for this is My body, which shall be broken for you. Do this in remembrance of Me.
Great is the mystery of faith. Great is the dignity and the eminent goodness of God towards us in this most holy Sacrament.
Strengthen me with heavenly bread, and refresh me with that divine cup, so that through Thy sacrament I may experience healing of both body and soul.
Great is the dignity of a priest, to whom it is given to consecrate the Lord of majesty with holy words, to praise Him with the lips, to take Him in the hands, to receive Him in the mouth.
I need not be present in bodily form, but where I am spiritually there am I wholly present. And I am near to all who call upon Me in truth.
When thou comest to receive this saving and divine sacrament, come as a thirsty man who longeth for cool water, as one who longeth to be cured of his sickness.
He that hath Me hath all things, and he that loseth Me loseth all. Without Me thou art nothing; without Me thou canst do nothing good.
Vouchsafe to look upon me from the excellency of Thy greatness, and to hear the prayer of Thy poor servant exiled far from Thee in the land of the shadow of death.
I am all and every thing to thee; and I desire that thou be altogether Mine, and that thou seek Me above thyself and above all things.
O how great peace and rest should he have, who would cast off all vain solicitude, and think only of divine things and things profitable for the soul, and would place all his trust in God!
How great is the abundance of Thy sweetness, O Lord, which Thou hast laid up for them that fear Thee! How excellent is the state of those who love Thee above all things!
My soul longeth and thirsteth to be with Thee, and to be united to Thee by the bonds of love and the joy of those who truly love Thee.
Many are the words, but few are the works. He who knoweth himself and holdeth himself vile, is pleasing to God and not displeasing.
Of what use is it to discourse learnedly on the Trinity, if thou be void of humility, and be thus displeasing to the Trinity?
He is truly great who hath great charity, and who thinketh little of himself, however honourable his rank may be.
My son, the nature of man is deficient in goodness, but grace is the great corrector of all that nature hath made faulty.
My son, I have spoken concerning many great things; do not be overcome by smallness of mind, but rise with great courage, and be no more cast down.
Neither doth the beauty of angels delight me, if Thou be absent, O Lord my God.
It is a great wisdom to know how to be silent and to look at neither the judge's unrighteousness nor his ignorance, and to wait upon God.
He to whom the Word of all things speaketh is free from many opinions, and at peace with himself.
Joy followeth sorrow, and the night is turned into the brightness of day for those who have hope in God.
My son, give up thy desire to know many things; for much distraction and deceit are therein.
How great is the difference between a man who thinketh much of God and one who thinketh little of Him; between him who loveth God and him who loveth the world!
